    GTSOM Tutorial: Mapping MNIST Handwritten Digits¶

    What is a Self-Organizing Map?¶

    A Self-Organizing Map (SOM) is an unsupervised learning algorithm that projects high-dimensional data onto a low-dimensional output lattice — typically a 2D grid — while preserving the topological structure of the data. Neurons that are nearby in the output lattice learn to respond to similar inputs, so the map can be read as a kind of 'neighborhood diagram' of the data: regions of the lattice that are close together represent data that are similar in the original high-dimensional space.

    Unlike dimensionality reduction methods such as PCA or UMAP, the SOM output is a fixed, structured lattice whose geometry is chosen by the user. This makes the result straightforward to visualize and interpret, and allows the user to impose domain-specific structure on the output space.

    GTSOM (General Topology SOM) extends the classical SOM by allowing arbitrary output space geometries, defined by any graph structure — hexagonal grids, rectangular grids, or data-driven Delaunay triangulations built from the data itself. This tutorial demonstrates the hexagonal grid constructor (GTSOM.from_grid) on the MNIST handwritten digits dataset.

    What we will do¶

    1. Load and preprocess MNIST (10,000 images, scaled to [0,1], reduced to 50 principal components)
    2. Initialize a 20×20 hexagonal GTSOM
    3. Train for 200 epochs with an exponentially decaying neighborhood bandwidth
    4. Visualize the learned map: digit organization, quantization error, and prototype images

    1. Imports¶

    In [1]:
    Copied! 
    import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
from matplotlib.patches import Patch
from sklearn.datasets import fetch_openml
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler

from gtsom import GTSOM

%matplotlib inline
plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120
    import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec from matplotlib.patches import Patch from sklearn.datasets import fetch_openml from sklearn.decomposition import PCA from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler from gtsom import GTSOM %matplotlib inline plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120

    2. Loading MNIST¶

    MNIST contains 70,000 28×28 grayscale images of handwritten digits (0–9), each flattened to a 784-dimensional vector. We use the last 10,000 samples, which form the standard test split and are approximately balanced across all 10 digit classes.

    Pixel values are scaled globally to [0, 1] before any further processing. GTSOM does not perform any internal scaling — preprocessing is the user's responsibility, and consistent scaling ensures that all input dimensions contribute equally to the nearest-neighbor search.

    In [2]:
    Copied! 
    # Fetch MNIST from OpenML (downloads ~11 MB on first run, cached thereafter)
mnist = fetch_openml('mnist_784', version=1, as_frame=False, parser='auto')
X_raw = mnist.data.astype(np.float32)   # (70000, 784)
y_raw = mnist.target.astype(int)         # (70000,)

# Use the last 10,000 samples — the standard balanced test split
N = 10_000
X_raw = X_raw[-N:]
y     = y_raw[-N:]

print(f'Dataset: {X_raw.shape}')
print(f'Label counts: { {d: int((y==d).sum()) for d in range(10)} }')

# Scale globally to [0, 1]
scaler   = MinMaxScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_raw).astype(np.float32)
print(f'Scaled range: [{X_scaled.min():.3f}, {X_scaled.max():.3f}]')
    # Fetch MNIST from OpenML (downloads ~11 MB on first run, cached thereafter) mnist = fetch_openml('mnist_784', version=1, as_frame=False, parser='auto') X_raw = mnist.data.astype(np.float32) # (70000, 784) y_raw = mnist.target.astype(int) # (70000,) # Use the last 10,000 samples — the standard balanced test split N = 10_000 X_raw = X_raw[-N:] y = y_raw[-N:] print(f'Dataset: {X_raw.shape}') print(f'Label counts: { {d: int((y==d).sum()) for d in range(10)} }') # Scale globally to [0, 1] scaler = MinMaxScaler() X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_raw).astype(np.float32) print(f'Scaled range: [{X_scaled.min():.3f}, {X_scaled.max():.3f}]')
    Dataset: (10000, 784)
Label counts: {0: 980, 1: 1135, 2: 1032, 3: 1010, 4: 982, 5: 892, 6: 958, 7: 1028, 8: 974, 9: 1009}
Scaled range: [0.000, 1.000]

    3. Dimensionality Reduction via PCA¶

    With d=784 input dimensions, computing nearest-neighbor distances for each data point at every training epoch would be expensive and dominated by uninformative dimensions (the blank border pixels common to all images). We reduce to 50 principal components, which captures approximately 85% of the total variance while dramatically reducing computation time.

    This PCA is a preprocessing step applied to the raw pixel data. The SOM then operates in this 50-dimensional PCA score space.

    In [3]:
    Copied! 
    N_PCS = 50
pca   = PCA(n_components=N_PCS, svd_solver='randomized', random_state=42)
X     = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled).astype(np.float32)
print(f'Reduced: {X_scaled.shape} -> {X.shape}')
print(f'Variance explained by {N_PCS} PCs: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum()*100:.1f}%')
    N_PCS = 50 pca = PCA(n_components=N_PCS, svd_solver='randomized', random_state=42) X = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled).astype(np.float32) print(f'Reduced: {X_scaled.shape} -> {X.shape}') print(f'Variance explained by {N_PCS} PCs: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum()*100:.1f}%')
    Reduced: (10000, 784) -> (10000, 50)
Variance explained by 50 PCs: 83.1%
    In [4]:
    Copied! 
    # Scree plot: cumulative variance explained
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
cumvar = np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_) * 100
ax.plot(range(1, N_PCS + 1), cumvar, 'o-', ms=3, lw=1.5, color='steelblue')
ax.axhline(85, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='85% variance')
ax.axhline(95, color='coral',   lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='95% variance')
ax.set_xlabel('Number of principal components')
ax.set_ylabel('Cumulative variance explained (%)')
ax.set_title('MNIST PCA — Cumulative Variance Explained')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    # Scree plot: cumulative variance explained fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3)) cumvar = np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_) * 100 ax.plot(range(1, N_PCS + 1), cumvar, 'o-', ms=3, lw=1.5, color='steelblue') ax.axhline(85, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='85% variance') ax.axhline(95, color='coral', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='95% variance') ax.set_xlabel('Number of principal components') ax.set_ylabel('Cumulative variance explained (%)') ax.set_title('MNIST PCA — Cumulative Variance Explained') ax.legend(fontsize=8) ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

    4. Choosing the Output Lattice¶

    Lattice size¶

    A common rule of thumb for SOM size is M ≈ 5√N neurons, where N is the number of training samples. For N=10,000 this gives M ≈ 500, suggesting a grid of roughly 22×22. We use a 20×20 grid (M=400), which is slightly more conservative but trains faster and remains visually interpretable. With 400 neurons and 10,000 data points, each neuron will serve on average 25 data points in its receptive field.

    Too few neurons: multiple digit classes are forced to share prototypes, blurring the map. Too many neurons: training is slow and many neurons end up with empty receptive fields.

    Hexagonal vs rectangular grid¶

    We use a hexagonal grid (coord_init='hex') rather than rectangular. In a hex grid every interior neuron has exactly 6 equidistant neighbors, giving a more uniform neighborhood structure than a rectangular grid where diagonal neighbors are further away than axis-aligned ones. This tends to produce smoother, more isotropic maps.

    Weight initialization¶

    W_init='random' initializes each prototype by sampling a random data point from X. This gives a starting configuration that is well-distributed within the data cloud. The SOM training then moves prototypes from these initial positions into the regions of the data space they will ultimately represent.

    5. Initialising the GTSOM¶

    In [5]:
    Copied! 
    SEED = 42

som = GTSOM(
    rho_0=10.0,
    rho_f=1.0,
    halflife_epochs=100,
    n_jobs=-1,
    nbr_topo_alpha_0=1.0,
    nbr_topo_alpha_f=1.0,
    random_state=SEED,
    compute_dr_metrics=True,
)
som.from_grid(
    X,
    shape=(20, 20),
    coord_init='hex',
    W_init='random',
    labels=y,
)
print(som)
print(f'Prototypes with empty receptive fields at init: '
      f'{(som.recaller.RFSize == 0).sum()} / {som.M}')
    SEED = 42 som = GTSOM( rho_0=10.0, rho_f=1.0, halflife_epochs=100, n_jobs=-1, nbr_topo_alpha_0=1.0, nbr_topo_alpha_f=1.0, random_state=SEED, compute_dr_metrics=True, ) som.from_grid( X, shape=(20, 20), coord_init='hex', W_init='random', labels=y, ) print(som) print(f'Prototypes with empty receptive fields at init: ' f'{(som.recaller.RFSize == 0).sum()} / {som.M}') 
    GTSOM(M=400, d=50, embed_dim=2, fitted=False, age=0, snapshots=1, backend='numba', n_jobs=16, nbr_influence_min=0.01, proto_topo='CONN_GD', alpha_schedule=ExponentialAnneal(initial=1.0, final=1.0, tau=200, flat=True), compute_dr_metrics=True, train_time=0.000s)
Prototypes with empty receptive fields at init: 0 / 400

    At initialization, prototypes are placed randomly within the data cloud and the output lattice has no meaningful organization — digit labels are distributed randomly across the hex grid, as the plot below shows.

    In [6]:
    Copied! 
    g = som.plot(
    color_by='labels',
    title='GTSOM — MNIST (20×20 hex)',
    subtitle='Initial state (age=0)',
)
g.draw()
    g = som.plot( color_by='labels', title='GTSOM — MNIST (20×20 hex)', subtitle='Initial state (age=0)', ) g.draw()
    Out[6]:

    6. The Learning Schedule¶

    Before training, we configure the neighborhood bandwidth schedule — the parameter ρ (rho) that controls how widely each prototype update propagates across the lattice.

    How the SOM update rule works¶

    At each training epoch, every data point is assigned to its nearest prototype (the Best Matching Unit, or BMU). The BMU and its neighbors are then pulled toward the data points assigned to the BMU. The neighborhood influence of prototype j on prototype b's update is determined by a kernel weight h(dist(b,j), ρ) that falls off with geodesic distance on the lattice. A large ρ means a wide neighborhood: many prototypes move together, producing coarse global organization. A small ρ means only immediate neighbors are affected, producing fine local refinement.

    Parameter choices¶

    rho_0 = 10.0 — initial bandwidth. The 20×20 hex lattice has a diameter of approximately 20 geodesic hops. Starting with ρ=10 means each prototype's neighborhood initially spans roughly half the lattice, ensuring the first training epochs establish global organization rather than local rearrangement.

    rho_f = 1.0 — final bandwidth. At ρ=1, only immediately adjacent neurons (6 neighbors in a hex grid) influence each other's updates. This is the standard SOM minimum: the map continues to refine locally but the global topology is fixed.

    halflife_epochs = 100. The bandwidth decays exponentially from ρ₀ to ρ_f; halflife_epochs sets the half-life of this decay (the geometric midpoint between ρ₀ and ρ_f is reached at halflife_epochs, and ρ_f is reached at 2 × halflife_epochs). With 200 total epochs and halflife_epochs=100, the bandwidth is actively decaying throughout the first half of training and at its floor for the second half — a natural two-phase structure: global organization followed by local refinement.

    n_jobs=-1 enables parallel prototype updates using all available CPU cores via a numba JIT-compiled kernel, significantly accelerating training on large datasets.

    In [7]:
    Copied! 
    # Visualize the bandwidth decay schedule
rho_vals = som.rho_schedule.values(200)
fig, ax  = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
ax.plot(range(200), rho_vals, lw=2, color='steelblue')
ax.axhline(1.0,  color='coral',   lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='rho_f = 1.0')
ax.axvline(100,  color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='halflife_epochs = 100')
ax.set_xlabel('Epoch')
ax.set_ylabel('Neighborhood bandwidth ρ')
ax.set_title('Exponential bandwidth decay schedule')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    # Visualize the bandwidth decay schedule rho_vals = som.rho_schedule.values(200) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3)) ax.plot(range(200), rho_vals, lw=2, color='steelblue') ax.axhline(1.0, color='coral', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='rho_f = 1.0') ax.axvline(100, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='halflife_epochs = 100') ax.set_xlabel('Epoch') ax.set_ylabel('Neighborhood bandwidth ρ') ax.set_title('Exponential bandwidth decay schedule') ax.legend(fontsize=8) ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

    7. Training¶

    We train for 200 epochs. A snapshot of the SOM state is captured every 25 epochs and stored in learn_history_ for retrospective inspection. The verbose output below shows the epoch number, current bandwidth ρ, global mean quantization error (MQE), and the proportion of data points whose nearest prototype changed since the previous epoch (delBMU).

    In [8]:
    Copied! 
    som.fit(
    X,
    n_epochs=200,
    labels=y,
    verbose=True,
    plot_every=25,
)
print(f'\nFinal state: {som}')
print(f'Empty RFs after training: {(som.recaller.RFSize == 0).sum()} / {som.M}')
    som.fit( X, n_epochs=200, labels=y, verbose=True, plot_every=25, ) print(f'\nFinal state: {som}') print(f'Empty RFs after training: {(som.recaller.RFSize == 0).sum()} / {som.M}')
      Epoch      rho    alpha        MQE    delBMU

Final state: GTSOM(M=400, d=50, embed_dim=2, fitted=True, age=200, snapshots=201, backend='numba', n_jobs=16, nbr_influence_min=0.01, proto_topo='CONN_GD', alpha_schedule=ExponentialAnneal(initial=1.0, final=1.0, tau=200, flat=True), compute_dr_metrics=True, train_time=143.837s)
Empty RFs after training: 0 / 400

    8. Learning Curves¶

    Two quantities are monitored at every epoch:

    Mean Quantization Error (MQE) is the root-mean-square Euclidean distance from each data point to its nearest prototype (i.e. the square root of the mean squared distance, not the mean distance itself — the standard RMSE-based definition of MQE in the SOM literature). It measures how well the prototypes represent the data — lower is better. MQE generally decreases monotonically as training progresses.

    delBMU is the proportion of data points whose nearest prototype (BMU) changed from the previous epoch. It measures how much the map is still reorganizing — a value near 0 indicates convergence. The periodic spikes visible in the delBMU curve reflect neighborhood shell collapses: as ρ decays, the kernel weight for prototypes at distance d geodesic hops drops below a minimum threshold, effectively removing that shell from the neighborhood. Each such collapse triggers a brief burst of BMU reassignments before the map re-settles.

    In [9]:
    Copied! 
    ages_arr   = np.array([s['age']    for s in som.learn_history_])
mqes_arr   = np.array([s['mqe']    for s in som.learn_history_])
delbmu_arr = np.array([s['delBMU'] for s in som.learn_history_])

fig, (ax_mqe, ax_del) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))

ax_mqe.plot(ages_arr, mqes_arr, 'o-', lw=1.5, ms=3, color='steelblue')
ax_mqe.axvline(0, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='Init (age=0)')
ax_mqe.set_xlabel('Age (epochs)')
ax_mqe.set_ylabel('Global MQE')
ax_mqe.set_title('SOM Learning\nGlobal MQE')
ax_mqe.legend(fontsize=8)
ax_mqe.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

ax_del.plot(ages_arr, delbmu_arr, 'o-', lw=1.5, ms=3, color='darkorange')
ax_del.axvline(0, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='Init (age=0)')
ax_del.set_xlabel('Age (epochs)')
ax_del.set_ylabel('delBMU')
ax_del.set_title('SOM Learning\nProportion of changed BMUs')
ax_del.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.05)
ax_del.legend(fontsize=8)
ax_del.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    ages_arr = np.array([s['age'] for s in som.learn_history_]) mqes_arr = np.array([s['mqe'] for s in som.learn_history_]) delbmu_arr = np.array([s['delBMU'] for s in som.learn_history_]) fig, (ax_mqe, ax_del) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4)) ax_mqe.plot(ages_arr, mqes_arr, 'o-', lw=1.5, ms=3, color='steelblue') ax_mqe.axvline(0, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='Init (age=0)') ax_mqe.set_xlabel('Age (epochs)') ax_mqe.set_ylabel('Global MQE') ax_mqe.set_title('SOM Learning\nGlobal MQE') ax_mqe.legend(fontsize=8) ax_mqe.grid(True, alpha=0.3) ax_del.plot(ages_arr, delbmu_arr, 'o-', lw=1.5, ms=3, color='darkorange') ax_del.axvline(0, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='Init (age=0)') ax_del.set_xlabel('Age (epochs)') ax_del.set_ylabel('delBMU') ax_del.set_title('SOM Learning\nProportion of changed BMUs') ax_del.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.05) ax_del.legend(fontsize=8) ax_del.grid(True, alpha=0.3) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

    9. Lattice Organization Over Time¶

    The following four panels show the SOM lattice colored by the plurality digit label at each neuron, captured at ages 0, 50, 100, and 200. At age 0 the labels are randomly distributed. By age 50 a coarse global structure is already visible — similar digits are beginning to cluster spatially. The map continues to sharpen through the remaining epochs as the neighborhood bandwidth narrows and prototypes refine their local territories.

    In [10]:
    Copied! 
    import io
from PIL import Image as PILImage

target_ages = [0, 50, 100, 200]
snap_by_age = {s['age']: s for s in som.learn_history_ if s['fig'] is not None}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 12))
fig.suptitle('GTSOM — MNIST lattice organization over training',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', y=1.01)

for ax, age in zip(axes.flat, target_ages):
    snap_fig = snap_by_age[age]['fig']  # plotnine ggplot object
    # Save ggplot to an in-memory PNG, then display with imshow
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    snap_fig.save(buf, format='png', dpi=100, width=6, height=5)
    buf.seek(0)
    img = PILImage.open(buf)
    ax.imshow(img)
    ax.axis('off')
    ax.set_title(f'Age = {age}', fontsize=12, pad=6)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    import io from PIL import Image as PILImage target_ages = [0, 50, 100, 200] snap_by_age = {s['age']: s for s in som.learn_history_ if s['fig'] is not None} fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 12)) fig.suptitle('GTSOM — MNIST lattice organization over training', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', y=1.01) for ax, age in zip(axes.flat, target_ages): snap_fig = snap_by_age[age]['fig'] # plotnine ggplot object # Save ggplot to an in-memory PNG, then display with imshow buf = io.BytesIO() snap_fig.save(buf, format='png', dpi=100, width=6, height=5) buf.seek(0) img = PILImage.open(buf) ax.imshow(img) ax.axis('off') ax.set_title(f'Age = {age}', fontsize=12, pad=6) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

    10. Final Learned Map¶

    Digit organization¶

    The map below shows the final trained SOM colored by the plurality digit label at each neuron. The SOM has learned a spatially coherent organization of the digit classes — similar-looking digits occupy neighboring regions of the lattice.

    The topology reflects the visual similarity structure of the digits: curved digits (0, 6, 8, 9) tend to cluster together, as do straight-stroked digits (1, 4, 7). Digits with high intra-class variability (notably 5, which can resemble 3, 6, or 8 depending on the writer) may appear scattered or occupy boundary regions between other digit clusters.

    In [11]:
    Copied! 
    g = som.plot(
    color_by='labels',
    title='GTSOM — MNIST (20×20 hex)',
    subtitle=f'age={som.age}, color_by=\'labels\'',
)
g.draw()
    g = som.plot( color_by='labels', title='GTSOM — MNIST (20×20 hex)', subtitle=f'age={som.age}, color_by=\'labels\'', ) g.draw()
    Out[11]:

    Quantization error and receptive field size¶

    Two further diagnostic views of the trained map:

    Mean Quantization Error per neuron (color_by='mqe') shows where the map fits the data well (low MQE, dark) and where prototypes are further from their assigned data points (high MQE, bright). High-MQE regions often correspond to class boundaries where a single prototype must represent data from multiple nearby clusters.

    Receptive field size (color_by='rfsize') shows how many data points are assigned to each neuron. A uniform distribution across neurons is ideal; neurons with very large receptive fields are 'overloaded' and may benefit from a larger lattice.

    In [12]:
    Copied! 
    for color_by in ['mqe', 'rfsize']:
    g = som.plot(
        color_by=color_by,
        title='GTSOM — MNIST',
        subtitle=f'color_by=\'{color_by}\'',
    )
    g.draw()
    for color_by in ['mqe', 'rfsize']: g = som.plot( color_by=color_by, title='GTSOM — MNIST', subtitle=f'color_by=\'{color_by}\'', ) g.draw()

    11. Prototype Images¶

    Each neuron's weight vector W[j] is a point in 50-dimensional PCA score space. By applying the inverse PCA transform, we can reconstruct the 28×28 pixel image that each neuron has learned to represent — the 'template' for its region of the digit space. Arranging these prototype images on the hex lattice produces a visual summary of the entire learned map.

    Prototypes in the interior of a digit cluster should resemble that digit clearly; prototypes at boundaries between clusters will show blended or ambiguous forms — a natural consequence of the SOM's smooth topographic mapping.

    In [13]:
    Copied! 
    # Back-project W from PCA score space to pixel space
W_pixels = pca.inverse_transform(som.W)          # (M, 784)
W_pixels = scaler.inverse_transform(W_pixels)    # undo [0,1] scaling
W_pixels = np.clip(W_pixels, 0, 255).astype(np.uint8)

coords  = som.embed.coords   # (M, 2) hex lattice positions
tile    = 28
x_min, x_max = coords[:, 0].min(), coords[:, 0].max()
y_min, y_max = coords[:, 1].min(), coords[:, 1].max()
x_range = x_max - x_min
y_range = y_max - y_min

canvas_w = int(20 * tile * 1.15)
canvas_h = int(20 * tile * 1.25)
canvas   = np.ones((canvas_h, canvas_w), dtype=np.uint8) * 255

for m in range(som.M):
    px = int((coords[m, 0] - x_min) / x_range * (canvas_w - tile))
    py = int((coords[m, 1] - y_min) / y_range * (canvas_h - tile))
    py = canvas_h - tile - py   # flip y
    py = max(0, min(py, canvas_h - tile))
    px = max(0, min(px, canvas_w - tile))
    canvas[py:py+tile, px:px+tile] = W_pixels[m].reshape(28, 28)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 10))
ax.imshow(canvas, cmap='gray_r', vmin=0, vmax=255)
ax.axis('off')
ax.set_title(
    'GTSOM — MNIST Prototype Images\n'
    '(each neuron\'s weight vector back-projected to pixel space)',
    fontsize=11,
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    # Back-project W from PCA score space to pixel space W_pixels = pca.inverse_transform(som.W) # (M, 784) W_pixels = scaler.inverse_transform(W_pixels) # undo [0,1] scaling W_pixels = np.clip(W_pixels, 0, 255).astype(np.uint8) coords = som.embed.coords # (M, 2) hex lattice positions tile = 28 x_min, x_max = coords[:, 0].min(), coords[:, 0].max() y_min, y_max = coords[:, 1].min(), coords[:, 1].max() x_range = x_max - x_min y_range = y_max - y_min canvas_w = int(20 * tile * 1.15) canvas_h = int(20 * tile * 1.25) canvas = np.ones((canvas_h, canvas_w), dtype=np.uint8) * 255 for m in range(som.M): px = int((coords[m, 0] - x_min) / x_range * (canvas_w - tile)) py = int((coords[m, 1] - y_min) / y_range * (canvas_h - tile)) py = canvas_h - tile - py # flip y py = max(0, min(py, canvas_h - tile)) px = max(0, min(px, canvas_w - tile)) canvas[py:py+tile, px:px+tile] = W_pixels[m].reshape(28, 28) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 10)) ax.imshow(canvas, cmap='gray_r', vmin=0, vmax=255) ax.axis('off') ax.set_title( 'GTSOM — MNIST Prototype Images\n' '(each neuron\'s weight vector back-projected to pixel space)', fontsize=11, ) plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

    12. Summary¶

    In this tutorial we trained a 20×20 hexagonal GTSOM on 10,000 MNIST digit images reduced to 50 principal components. The trained map shows clear topographic organization: digit classes occupy contiguous regions of the lattice, and the spatial relationships between regions reflect the visual similarity of the digits.

    Key parameters and their roles:

    Parameter Value Role
    Grid shape 20×20 hex M=400 neurons; balanced coverage for N=10,000
    W_init 'random' Prototypes initialized from random data points
    rho_0 10.0 Initial bandwidth ≈ half the lattice diameter
    rho_f 1.0 Final bandwidth = single-neighbor influence
    halflife_epochs 100 Bandwidth half-life; ρ_f reached at epoch 200
    n_epochs 200 Total training epochs

    Next steps¶

    • Data-driven topology: GTSOM.from_data builds the output lattice via Delaunay triangulation of prototype positions in PCA or Laplacian Eigenmap space, allowing the topology to adapt to the data rather than being fixed in advance. See the companion notebook, GTSOM Tutorial: General Topology Learning on Synthetic Blobs, for a worked example.
    • Larger grids: For exploratory analysis of large datasets, a 30×30 or 40×40 grid provides finer spatial resolution at the cost of longer training time.
    • Parallelism: n_jobs, set in the GTSOM constructor, controls the number of CPU threads used for prototype updates. On multi-core machines, n_jobs=-1 (all cores) can reduce training time substantially for large N and d.
    Made with Material for MkDocs
